---
title: "Lab 6"
subtitle: "Distribution Forensics: What the Mean Hides in a GreenBowl A/B Test"
author: "Ihor Miroshnychenko"
institute: Kyiv School of Economics
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: true
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

# Objective

In **Lab 4** you ran a two-sample $t$-test on ARPU and recovered a business
decision. In **Lab 5** you went one level up and *audited the criterion*
itself with Monte Carlo. **Lab 6 is forensics: a clean $t$-test verdict is not
enough, because the $t$-test only ever looks at the mean.**

The GreenBowl experimentation platform shipped you the results of a new-menu
A/B test, and the automated report shows a tidy $t$-test "ship / don't ship"
line. Your job is to decide whether you can **believe** it. The lecture gave
you a toolkit where everything reduces to one idea — **compare cumulative
distribution functions** — and several of those tools see things the mean
cannot:

- **Shapiro–Wilk + QQ-plot** — is the metric even *normal* enough to trust the
  $t$-test on a small sample?
- **Two-sample Kolmogorov–Smirnov** — was the **randomization honest** (the
  A/A guardrail on the pre-period), and did the treatment change the
  **distribution** where the mean stayed flat?
- **Empirical CDF** — *where* and *how* do the two groups differ?
- **Monte Carlo** — is your own guardrail actually calibrated on skewed money
  data?

The twist, as in Labs 4–5: **you do not get to see what is wrong.** Your
experiment is pulled live from `greenbowl_gof.py`, keyed to your student id.
Some students received an honest experiment with a real mean lift; some
received one where the effect is hidden in the *shape*; some received a broken
randomization, a wave of churned users, a metric that must be analysed on the
log scale, or a *paired* design masquerading as two independent groups. **The
only honest way to find out which one you hold is to run the diagnostics.**

You will:

1. Pull your experiment and **diagnose the metric's distribution** (Shapiro +
   QQ), deciding whether to work on the raw or the log scale.
2. Build a small, reusable `dist_guardrail` toolkit step by step and run the
   **A/A guardrail** (two-sample KS on the pre-period) to test whether the
   randomization is honest.
3. Ask whether the effect lives in the **shape**, not the mean — comparing the
   $t$-test against KS + the ECDF on the experiment period.
4. Run the **independence check** and, if your data are paired, redo the
   analysis the right way (per-user differences).
5. **Validate your guardrail with Monte Carlo** — its FPR, the minimum $n$,
   and its power on your kind of skewed data.
6. Deliver a one-page **forensic verdict** that a non-statistician PM can act
   on.

# General Requirements

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## The fixed business context (same for everyone)

- **Metric:** weekly **ARPU** per user, in UAH — continuous, positive,
  right-skewed (lognormal).
- **Baseline:** $\mu_0 = 250$ UAH.
- **Cohorts:** `control` = old menu, `test` = new menu. Each arm also has a
  **pre-period** sample (`pre_control`, `pre_test`) from *before* the treatment
  started — when, by construction, no effect can exist.
- **Direction of interest:** we would ship only if the new menu is *better* →
  one-sided thinking, $H_1: \mu_{\text{test}} > \mu_{\text{control}}$, but
  distributional effects can go any way.
- **Significance level:** $\alpha = 0.05$.
:::

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Everyone's experiment is different — on purpose

Your experiment is unique to your id. It might contain a clean mean lift, an
effect hidden in the shape, a randomization bug, zero-inflation (churn), a
small-sample non-normal metric, or a paired design. **There is no single
"right answer" shared across the class** — there is only the diagnosis your
diagnostics support. Copying another student's conclusion will be obviously
wrong for your experiment.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Report Format

Submit the report in **one** of the following formats:

- **Quarto → PDF** (`.qmd` → `.pdf`)
- **Quarto → HTML** (`.qmd` → `.html`, hosted, e.g. on
  [Quarto Pub](https://quartopub.com/),
  [Posit Connect Cloud](https://connect.posit.cloud/))
- **Jupyter Notebook** (`.ipynb`)
- **Google Colab** (share the link with viewing permissions)

The report must contain both code **and** narrative. Every section begins with
a written explanation, not code.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## What you build vs. what you use ready-made

The thing you **build** today is the **`dist_guardrail` toolkit**
(`check_normality`, `same_distribution`, and the Monte-Carlo validators) —
grown step by step from Practice 6. The **criteria** themselves you take
ready-made: `scipy.stats.shapiro`, `scipy.stats.ks_2samp`,
`scipy.stats.kstest`, `scipy.stats.probplot`, and
`statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportion_confint` for the Wilson interval. You
may **not** replace your validators with a library power/AA function — the
simulation loop and the confidence-interval verdict must be **your own code**.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Honour rule on the data platform

`greenbowl_gof.py` contains the hidden truth in its source. **Do not read past
the `# === instructor section ===` line, and do not call `_reveal`.** The point
of the lab is to reach the diagnosis *by running the diagnostics*, not by
reading the answer. Any report that names the hidden pathology from the source
instead of demonstrating it with the toolkit scores **zero** for
interpretation.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Deadline

📅 **June 24, 2026, 23:59**
:::

# Tasks

## Get Your Experiment {#sec-data}

Download `greenbowl_gof.py` (provided with the lab) into your working folder
and load **your** experiment. Use **one** id consistently — your KSE email is
recommended.

```{python}
#| label: load
#| eval: false

from greenbowl_gof import load_my_experiment, load_my_log, arpu_sample, BASELINE, ALPHA
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats
from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportion_confint

STUDENT_ID = "your.email@kse.org.ua"   # <-- put YOUR id here
exp = load_my_experiment(STUDENT_ID)

print(exp)                                  # arm sizes
print(f"control mean = {exp.control.mean():.1f} ₴,  test mean = {exp.test.mean():.1f} ₴")
print(f"observed lift = {exp.test.mean() - exp.control.mean():+.1f} ₴")
print(f"baseline = {BASELINE} ₴,  alpha = {ALPHA}")
```

::: {.border}
1. State your `STUDENT_ID`. Report $n_{\text{control}}$, $n_{\text{test}}$, the
   two means, and the observed **lift**
   $\overline{\text{test}} - \overline{\text{control}}$.
2. Plot overlaid histograms of `control` and `test`, with vertical lines at the
   baseline $\mu_0 = 250$ and each sample mean. In one sentence, describe the
   shape you see.
3. Run the platform's **naive $t$-test**
   (`scipy.stats.ttest_ind(exp.test, exp.control, equal_var=False)`) and write
   down the "headline" verdict it would print. **This is the verdict you are
   about to put on trial** — do not believe it yet.
4. Confirm in one sentence that you understand the experiment's true nature is
   **hidden**, and your task is to *diagnose* it with goodness-of-fit tools.
:::

## Is the Metric Normal? (`check_normality`) {#sec-normal}

On a small sample the $t$-test relies on the **normality of the data** (a
large $n$ is rescued by the CLT). Before trusting any mean-based verdict, the
metric must be **checked** — and a QQ-plot shows *how* it deviates, which a
single $p$-value hides.

::: {.border}
1. Implement **Version 1** of your toolkit,
   `check_normality(sample, alpha=0.05)`, returning the Shapiro–Wilk $W$, its
   $p$-value, and a human-readable verdict (mirror Practice 6).
2. Run `check_normality` **and** a QQ-plot (`scipy.stats.probplot`) on your
   `control` arm, on the **raw** scale and on the **log** scale
   (`np.log`). Read the QQ-plots: where do the points leave the line?
3. **Mind the sample size.** If your $n$ is **large** (hundreds+), do *not*
   chase the Shapiro $p$-value — explain, in one sentence, why a normality test
   rejects "everything" at large $n$ and why you judge normality by **QQ + the
   CLT** here. If your $n$ is **small** (a few dozen), the test is meaningful —
   say what it tells you.
4. **Decide the scale.** Based on 2–3, state whether you will analyse this
   metric on the **raw** or the **log** scale, and what changes about the
   meaning of "the effect" if you log it (arithmetic vs. geometric mean). If
   your sample is small, **do not stop at the QQ-plot** — a non-normal shape
   does not by itself break the $t$-test (a balanced two-sample test is
   surprisingly robust to skew). Treat the scale choice as a *hypothesis* and
   confirm it in @sec-mc by checking whether the raw vs. log test is actually
   calibrated at **your** $n$ and **your** arm balance.
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Research hook
The log transform is the analyst's standard move for skewed money/time
metrics. If `check_normality` flips from "reject" on the raw scale to "do not
reject" on the log scale, that is a finding — but the QQ-plot alone does not
prove the raw test is broken. Skew bites hardest on a **small** *and*
**unbalanced** sample; on a balanced one the $t$-test largely shrugs it off.
Earn the log-scale verdict with the calibration check in @sec-mc, don't assume
it.
:::

## The A/A Guardrail: Was the Randomization Honest? (`same_distribution`) {#sec-aa}

Before believing **any** effect, check that the split itself was clean: an
honest randomization makes control and test share the **whole distribution**,
not just the mean. This is a **homogeneity** question — the two-sample KS — and
the right place to ask it is the **pre-period**, where no treatment effect can
exist yet.

::: {.border}
1. Implement **Version 2**, `same_distribution(x, y, alpha=0.05)`, using
   `scipy.stats.ks_2samp`, returning $D$, the $p$-value, and a verdict
   (mirror Practice 6).
2. Run it on the **pre-period**: `same_distribution(exp.pre_control,
   exp.pre_test)`. Plot the two empirical CDFs on one axis.
3. **Interpret as a guardrail.** A rejection on the pre-period is an alarm —
   no effect can exist there, so it means the groups were **not** comparable to
   begin with (a splitter / SRM-by-shape bug). State clearly: **does your
   pre-period A/A pass or fail**, and what that means for whether the
   experiment result can be trusted at all.
:::

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Stop-the-line rule
If the **pre-period A/A fails**, the experiment-period comparison is
**meaningless** until the randomization is fixed — a difference you see later
cannot be attributed to the treatment. If it fails, say so plainly: the rest of
your analysis becomes "here is the bug", not "here is the effect".
:::

## Does the Effect Live in the Shape, Not the Mean? {#sec-shape}

The lecture's central thesis: the $t$-test answers only "**did the mean
move?**". An effect in **variance, tails, or the share of zeros** is invisible
to it but caught by **KS** + the **ECDF**. (Run this section only if your
pre-period A/A from @sec-aa **passed** — otherwise note that a broken split
makes the experiment comparison void, and treat the differences below as
evidence of the bug, not of an effect.)

::: {.border}
1. On the **experiment period**, run **both** criteria on the same arms:
   - the $t$-test on the mean
     (`scipy.stats.ttest_ind(..., equal_var=False)`), and
   - `same_distribution(exp.control, exp.test)` (KS on the whole CDF).
   Tabulate the two $p$-values.
2. Plot the two **empirical CDFs** of `control` vs. `test`. Annotate *where*
   they differ (a location shift? a fan-out at the tail? a jump at zero?).
3. **Diagnose the four possible patterns** and say which one you are in, with
   the evidence:
   - **$t$ rejects *and* KS rejects** → a genuine shift that also moves the
     mean (a clean win/loss);
   - **$t$ silent but KS rejects** → the effect is in the **shape** (variance /
     tails / zeros), not the mean — the mean-only report would miss it;
   - **$t$ rejects but KS does not** → a small mean shift on otherwise-identical
     shapes (rare here);
   - **both silent** → no detectable distributional change.
4. **Zero-inflation probe.** Compute the **share of zero-spend users** in each
   arm. If they differ, explain why the right tool for "share of zeros" is a
   **$z$-test for proportions** (run it), and why an ARPU mean can stay flat
   while users churn underneath it.
:::

## The Independence Check (Research) {#sec-paired}

The two-sample KS — and the whole "two groups" framing — assumes the arms are
**independent**. A classic A/B anti-pattern compares a metric **before** and
**after** on the **same** users, which makes the samples dependent and the
two-sample KS conservative and weak.

::: {.border}
1. **Check it.** Compare the user ids of the two arms: how many users appear in
   **both** `exp.uid_control` and `exp.uid_test`
   (`np.intersect1d`)? State whether your arms are **independent** (disjoint
   users) or **paired** (shared users).
2. **If independent:** confirm in one sentence that the two-sample KS in
   @sec-aa–@sec-shape was the right tool, and move on.
3. **If paired:** the two-sample KS was the **wrong** method. Redo the analysis
   on the **per-user differences** $d_i = \text{after}_i - \text{before}_i$
   (i.e. `exp.test - exp.control`, since the rows are aligned by user):
   - run `check_normality(d)` (or on $\log$ differences) and a one-sample test
     of $d$ against $0$;
   - show, side by side, that the naive two-sample KS **missed** the effect
     while the paired analysis **finds** it, and explain *why* (the shared
     user component glues the two CDFs together).
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Research hook
This is the part you must *discover*, not assume. Most ids are independent;
some are not. Reporting "I checked the user ids and they are disjoint, so the
two-sample test is valid" is a real finding — and skipping the check on a
paired dataset is a real mistake.
:::

## Validate Your Guardrail with Monte Carlo {#sec-mc}

Your KS guardrail is itself a **criterion**, so validate it as in Practice
6 / Lab 5: does it keep the promised 5% false positives on **skewed** ARPU
data, and **from what $n$**? Use `arpu_sample` as your "god" generator — you
set the truth, so you always know the right answer.

::: {.border}
1. Implement `ks_fpr(gen, n, n_exps=2000, alpha=0.05, seed=...)` that runs an
   **A/A** ($H_0$ true: both groups from the **same** `gen`) `n_exps` times and
   returns the FPR **with** a 95% **Wilson** CI
   (`proportion_confint(method="wilson")`).
2. **Calibration.** Run it for `arpu_sample(250, n)` at a small, a medium, and
   a large $n$ (e.g. 20 / 100 / 1000). Report each FPR with its CI and whether
   $\alpha = 0.05$ is inside. Plot FPR (with the Wilson band) vs. $n$ with a
   dashed line at $0.05$. Explain the small-$n$ behaviour you see (conservative?
   why?).
3. **Power for *your* effect.** Implement `ks_power(gen_c, gen_t, n, ...)` and
   measure the power of the KS guardrail to detect an effect **like the one in
   your experiment** (a mean shift, a shape/variance change, or a zero-spike —
   whichever matches your diagnosis from @sec-shape). How large an $n$ would you
   need for power $\ge 0.8$?
4. **Real-log A/A (optional but encouraged).** Load `load_my_log(STUDENT_ID)`,
   split each `(city, cuisine)` slice in half with an **independent** random
   permutation (no treatment → $H_0$ true), run your `same_distribution` on
   each, and report the A/A FPR with a Wilson CI. Does it match your synthetic
   calibration?
:::

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Match the validation to *your* diagnosis
The criterion you validate must be the one your verdict rests on:

- **Small / skewed sample (the "new city" case):** your verdict may hinge on
  the **$t$-test**, not KS — so validate *that*. Run the A/A FPR of the
  $t$-test on **raw vs. log** ARPU at **your $n$ and your arm balance**
  (`scipy.stats.ttest_ind`). On a small, *unbalanced* skewed design the raw
  test can be miscalibrated (FPR off $\alpha$) while the log-scale test is on
  target — that calibration gap, not just the QQ-plot, is what *justifies*
  working on the log scale. (Note: a *balanced* raw $t$-test is far more robust
  to skew — so check your own balance before concluding.)
- **Paired design (shared users):** the two-sample KS is the **wrong**
  guardrail here, so do not validate it as if it were right — validate the
  **paired** criterion you actually used in @sec-paired (a one-sample test on
  the differences $d_i$), and show it recovers power the naive two-sample KS
  throws away.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Why this matters
Whatever you conclude in @sec-aa–@sec-shape rests on the criterion being
trustworthy at *your* sample size. If your $n$ is small and the guardrail is
conservative there, a "pass" is weaker than it looks — say so. "Conservative"
(FPR below $\alpha$) is **safe but underpowered**; "invalid" (FPR above
$\alpha$) is **not**. Keep that distinction from Lab 5.
:::

## Final Forensic Verdict {#sec-final}

::: {.border}
Write a **single-page memo** (≤ 400 words) to a **non-technical** GreenBowl PM
who has only seen the platform's headline $t$-test line. It must contain:

1. The **headline $t$-test verdict** (what the automated report claimed) and
   your **one-line forensic verdict** — one of: a real mean win/loss; an effect
   hidden in the **shape**; a **broken randomization** (don't trust it); a
   **churn / zero-inflation** problem under a flat mean; a metric that must be
   read on the **log scale**; or a **paired** design analysed the wrong way.
2. The **evidence** that forces it, quoted as numbers, not adjectives: the
   normality decision (raw vs. log), the pre-period A/A result, the
   $t$-vs-KS comparison with the ECDF, the independence check, and the share of
   zeros if relevant.
3. A concrete **recommendation**: ship / don't ship / fix the splitter / report
   the distribution (not just the mean) / re-analyse as paired / collect more
   data — whichever your diagnosis demands.
4. One honest **caveat or limitation** of your own audit (e.g. KS is
   conservative at your $n$; KS is sensitive to *everything* and does not tell
   you whether the difference is business-significant; simulation noise at the
   `n_exps` you used).

It must be readable by someone who has never heard the words "Kolmogorov" or
"$p$-value". You are graded on whether the PM could act on it without
follow-up questions.
:::

# Grading {.unnumbered}

| Criterion | Points |
|---|:---:|
| Correctness of methodology | 3 |
| Interpretation of results | 3 |
| Code quality and organization | 2 |
| **Total** | **8** |

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Grading Details

**Correctness of methodology (3 pts):**

- `check_normality` and `same_distribution` are correct, and the
  normality / scale decision in @sec-normal is justified by the QQ-plot and the
  sample size (1 pt).
- The pre-period A/A guardrail and the experiment-period $t$-vs-KS comparison
  are run correctly with ECDF plots; the independence check in @sec-paired is
  performed and acted on (1 pt).
- The Monte-Carlo validation returns the FPR/power **with** a Wilson CI from a
  correct simulation loop, and is read for calibration vs. power (1 pt).

**Interpretation of results (3 pts):**

- @sec-normal correctly reads the large-$n$ vs. small-$n$ normality situation
  (CLT vs. log transform) instead of blindly trusting the Shapiro $p$-value.
- @sec-aa–@sec-shape reach the correct diagnosis (honest split vs. SRM; mean
  effect vs. shape effect vs. zero-inflation) with the right evidence, and the
  independence trap is handled correctly when present.
- The memo's forensic verdict **matches the evidence** and overturns or
  confirms the headline $t$-test for the right reason.

**Code quality and organization (2 pts):**

- `check_normality`, `same_distribution`, and the Monte-Carlo validators are
  single reusable functions used across all sections (built step by step, not
  re-implemented inline).
- Plots (histograms, QQ-plots, ECDFs, the FPR-vs-$n$ curve) have axis labels,
  titles, legends, and a dashed $\alpha$ line where relevant.
- Narrative precedes every code block; each section reads as an argument, not a
  dump of outputs.
:::

# Defense {.unnumbered}

During the defense (5–7 minutes) you will be asked *"What if?"* questions that
probe whether you understand **why** each piece exists, for example:

- *"Shapiro rejected normality on your data. Does that kill the $t$-test, or
  not? What single thing settles it — the $p$-value or something else?"*
- *"Your $t$-test said 'no effect' but your KS rejected. Explain to the PM, in
  one sentence, what changed in the experiment."*
- *"Your pre-period A/A failed. Why does that make the experiment-period result
  meaningless, no matter how small the $p$-value there is?"*
- *"Show me — live — how many users your two arms share. If they overlap, what
  must you do differently, and why does the two-sample KS mislead you here?"*
- *"Your KS guardrail's FPR came out at $0.032$ with CI $(0.026, 0.040)$ at
  $n=20$. Is it broken, conservative, or just noisy? Which, and how do you
  know?"*
- *"Your ARPU mean is flat but 8% of the test group spent zero. Why is a
  $z$-test for proportions the right tool there, and not KS?"*

You may also be asked to **change one thing live** (switch raw ↔ log, swap the
pre-period for the experiment period, shrink $n$ in the Monte-Carlo, reshuffle
the A/A split) and explain the resulting shift.
